overview.introduction: Docker is the industry-standard containerization platform. It packages applications and their dependencies into a single lightweight container, ensuring it runs identically on any environment.
overview.why_it_matters: Before Docker, deploying software was plagued by the 'works on my machine' problem. Different OS versions, libraries, and configurations caused production failures. Docker eliminates this entirely.
overview.industry_usage: Used daily by software and DevOps engineers to orchestrate microservices, accelerate CI/CD builds, isolate development environments, and run cloud-native scale workloads.
overview.what_you_will_build: A series of running containers including Nginx and interactive shells, configure environment variables, list container registries, and audit service logs.
learning_outcomes: Pull public images from Docker Hub.
Manage container lifecycles (run, stop, list, inspect, remove).
Configure environment variables in running container runtime.
Diagnose container state issues using log outputs.

key_concepts.title: Docker Client & Daemon
key_concepts.definition: Docker uses a client-server architecture. The Client is the CLI tool you type commands into. The Daemon (dockerd) is the background service that does the heavy lifting of building and running containers.
key_concepts.explanation: When you run 'docker run', the client sends a REST API request to the daemon, which pulls the image and launches the container.

commands.name: docker version
commands.output_explanation: Confirming both client and background daemon are active.

commands.name: docker info
commands.output_explanation: Shows count of running, paused, and stopped containers, alongside storage drivers.

key_concepts.title: Docker Images & Registries
key_concepts.definition: An Image is a read-only blueprint template with instructions for creating a Docker container. Registries (like Docker Hub) are online catalogs where images are uploaded and shared.
key_concepts.explanation: Think of an image as an installer package (like .msi or .dmg). You download it once, and can install it multiple times to create running containers.

commands.name: docker pull
commands.purpose: Download an image from a registry.
commands.expected_output: Status: Downloaded newer image for alpine:latest
commands.output_explanation: alpine is a ultra-lightweight Linux distribution image (under 8MB).

commands.name: docker images
commands.expected_output:
REPOSITORY    TAG       IMAGE ID       SIZE
alpine        latest    05455a08881e   7.38MB
commands.output_explanation: Shows repository name, tag version, image ID hash, and disk size.

key_concepts.title: Docker Container Lifecycle
key_concepts.definition: A Container is a runnable instance of an image. You can start, stop, pause, and delete containers using the Docker CLI.
key_concepts.explanation: Containers run in isolated sandboxes. When stopped, the process halts but container state is kept. When removed, all internal data changes are lost unless volumes are mounted.

commands.name: docker run
commands.purpose: Create and start a container from an image.
commands.output_explanation: Starts nginx web server in background (-d) naming it web_server, outputting the container ID.

commands.name: docker ps
commands.expected_output:
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE     COMMAND                  NAMES
a1b2c3d4e5f6   nginx     "/docker-entrypoint…"   web_server
commands.output_explanation: Shows container ID, image, command, status, ports, and name.

lessons.title: 1. Check Docker Version
lessons.definition: Confirming the Docker client is active and communicating with the host daemon.
lessons.explanation: Always verify tool status before deploying containers. Use --version flag to test connection.
lessons.instruction: Print the installed Docker version using 'docker --version'.

lessons.title: 2. Inspect System Configuration
lessons.definition: Querying system-wide setup options from the background Docker daemon.
lessons.explanation: docker info lists driver info, operating system, cpu counts, and storage allocations.
lessons.instruction: Query daemon system details using docker info.

lessons.title: 3. Pull Alpine Image
lessons.definition: Downloading a light Linux base image from Docker Hub.
lessons.instruction: Pull the latest alpine image from Docker Hub registry.

lessons.title: 4. List Cached Images
lessons.definition: Viewing locally available images in your machine storage.
lessons.instruction: List all images locally downloaded in this terminal.

lessons.title: 5. Run Hello-World Test
lessons.definition: Deploying a simple container to confirm sandboxing is functional.
lessons.instruction: Run a test hello-world container to test execution pipelines.

lessons.title: 6. Run Background Web Server
lessons.definition: Running web servers in detached state keeps the console active.
lessons.instruction: Run a detached Nginx container named 'my_web' in the background.

lessons.title: 7. List Active Containers
lessons.definition: Inspecting current container processes executing on the machine.
lessons.instruction: List running containers to verify 'my_web' status.

lessons.title: 8. Inspect Container Configuration
lessons.definition: Accessing metadata configurations of a container in JSON format.
lessons.instruction: Inspect the my_web container configuration detail using docker inspect.

exercises.title: Examine Stopped Containers
exercises.problem: Run a container using `docker run alpine echo 'hello'`, then run `docker ps` and `docker ps -a`. Explain the difference in output.
exercises.hint: The alpine container finishes its echo command immediately and exits. docker ps only lists running containers; docker ps -a lists both running and stopped ones.
exercises.expected_result: alpine is not listed in `docker ps` but is visible in `docker ps -a` with Status 'Exited (0)'.
exercises.objective: Understand the difference between active processes and exited container states.

exercises.title: Forcefully Stop a Web Service
exercises.problem: Launch an nginx container in detached mode, verify its status, stop it using `docker stop`, and verify it is no longer running.
exercises.hint: Use docker run -d --name temp_web nginx, then docker stop temp_web, then docker ps.
exercises.expected_result: temp_web appears in first list but is gone after running the stop command.

quiz.question: What is a Docker image?
options: A running instance of an isolated application.
A read-only blueprint containing code, libraries, and runtime dependencies.
A configuration file specifying environment ports.
quiz.answer: A read-only blueprint containing code, libraries, and runtime dependencies.
quiz.explanation: Images are read-only templates used to launch containers. Once instantiated, they become running containers.

quiz.question: Which Docker CLI command builds an image from a Dockerfile?
quiz.explanation: 'docker build' reads instructions inside a Dockerfile to package layers into a runnable image.

quiz.question: What does the -d flag represent in 'docker run -d nginx'?
options: Run the container in detached mode (background).
quiz.answer: Run the container in detached mode (background).
quiz.explanation: Detached mode runs container execution in the background, freeing your active shell console.

quiz.question: How do you view both running and stopped containers?
quiz.explanation: The -a (or --all) flag lists all containers in the system registry database, stopped or running.

quiz.question: Which command downloads an image from Docker Hub without running it?
quiz.explanation: 'docker pull' downloads the image tags from registries to local cache, saving build start times.

quiz.question: What is the difference between a container and an image?
options: A container is a virtual machine; an image is a script.
An image is a read-only template; a container is a live, writeable instance of that template.
Images run in background; containers run in foreground.
quiz.answer: An image is a read-only template; a container is a live, writeable instance of that template.
quiz.explanation: An image is the static blueprint package. The container is the running process context instantiated from it.

quiz.question: Which command removes a stopped container from disk?
quiz.explanation: 'docker rm' deletes container configurations. To delete images, use 'docker rmi'.

quiz.question: What happens to data inside a container when the container is deleted?
options: The data is archived in host log backups.
The data remains safe in cached image layers.
The data is permanently lost unless a volume was mounted.
The daemon prompts to save changes.
quiz.answer: The data is permanently lost unless a volume was mounted.
quiz.explanation: Containers are ephemeral; their write layers are destroyed on removal. Volumes are required to persist data.

quiz.question: Which command inspects metadata configurations of a container?
quiz.explanation: 'docker inspect' returns low-level system configuration arrays for Docker objects in JSON layout.

quiz.question: How do you specify a custom name for a container during startup?
quiz.explanation: Use the '--name' parameter option during docker run to assign a custom label.

interactive_examples.objective: Verify Docker client and daemon connectivity.
interactive_examples.scenario: You need to confirm that the Docker CLI tool can communicate with the background daemon.
interactive_examples.explanation: Docker client contacts the Docker daemon to retrieve system-wide info (running containers, images, OS version).
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Lists system summary including container statistics and driver info.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Standard health check command for scripts running inside automated container build agents.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Running without sudo/admin permissions when Docker daemon requires socket group permissions.
interactive_examples.tips: Use 'docker version' to inspect specific binary build details of client vs server.

interactive_examples.scenario: You need to debug a network issue or inspect directories inside a pristine Ubuntu environment.
interactive_examples.explanation: The -i (interactive) and -t (tty) flags attach standard input/output to launch an interactive shell terminal.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: You are placed inside an interactive shell prompt as root inside the container.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Ad-hoc debugging, testing packages, or analyzing configuration environments.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting to exit (using 'exit' or Ctrl+D) leaving running/stopped container resources behind.
interactive_examples.tips: Add '--rm' to automatically remove the container when it exits: docker run -it --rm alpine sh

interactive_examples.scenario: You want to run a web server in the background and test connections locally.
interactive_examples.explanation: -d runs detached in background, -p maps port 8080 on the host to 80 in container, --name assigns a custom name.
interactive_examples.output_explanation: Prints the full container ID hash indicating the container successfully launched in the background.
interactive_examples.real_world_use: Standard method to run web servers, APIs, and background processes in production.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Attempting to use a host port that is already in use by another application.
interactive_examples.tips: Run 'docker logs my_nginx' to inspect startup and request log stdout stream.

resources.summary: Master container runtimes, image pulls, volume configuration, port mapping, and system telemetry stats.

links.title: Docker Hub Official Repository
links.description: Public registry hosting official base images.

resources.cheat_sheet:
docker pull <image>          # Download image
docker run -d -p 80:80 <image> # Start container detached
docker ps -a                 # List all containers
docker logs <container>      # View stdout logs
docker stop <container>      # Halt running container
docker rm <container>        # Delete container metadata

commands_table.name: docker run
commands_table.description: Create and start a new container from an image.

commands_table.name: docker inspect
commands_table.description: Get detailed JSON metadata of a container/image.

revision_notes: Containers are running processes isolated by namespaces and control groups.
Images are static read-only templates. Containers are live writeable instances.
The host port is mapped first: -p host_port:container_port.
Docker Hub is the default registry for public images.
best_practices: Always specify tag versions (e.g. nginx:alpine) instead of using 'latest'.
Add --rm for short-lived debugging tasks to prevent disk space leaks.
Isolate config parameters using environment variables rather than baking them into images.
beginner_mistakes: Assuming data persists inside container file structures when it is deleted.
Using host ports that are already bound by local services.
Attempting to manage images using container commands (like docker rm instead of rmi).

interview_questions.question: What is the difference between an image and a container?
interview_questions.answer: An image is a static read-only blueprint containing binaries and code. A container is a dynamic, running runtime instance of that image.

interview_questions.question: How do you map host port 8080 to container port 80?

books.title: Docker Deep Dive — Nigel Poulton
books.description: Comprehensive reference covering core architectures and CLI commands.
